title: Convert units word problems (metrics)
description: Solve 1-step word problems involving metric units of measure and time.

assessment_items.question_content:
Gina made $9{,}350 \text{ mL}$ of lentil soup.  She and her kids ate $1.8 \text{ L}$ of the soup for lunch.

**How  many *milliliters* of soup did Gina have leftover?**  

[[☃ input-number 3]] $\text{mL}$


[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
First, we need to convert $\blueE{1.8}\text{ L}$ to milliliters.

$1 \text{ L} = 1{,}000\text{ mL}$.

So,

$\begin{align}
\blueE{1.8}\text{ L}&={\blueE{1.8}}\text{ L} \times {1{,}000}\text{ mL per L} \\\\
&= \blueE{1{,}800} \text{ mL}
\end{align}$

Now, we need to find out how much soup Gina had leftover.

Gina had a total of $\purpleE{9{,}350}\text{ mL}$ of soup. She and her kids ate $\blueE{1{,}800}\text{ mL}$ of soup.

[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueE{1{,}800}\text { mL}~ +~ \maroonD{?}\text { mL} = {}\purpleE{9{,}350}\text{ mL}$

Gina had $\maroonD{7{,}550}\text{ mL}$ of soup leftover.


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueE{1{,}800}\text { mL}~ +~ \maroonD{7{,}550}\text { mL} = {}\purpleE{9{,}350}\text{ mL}$
Gina had ${7{,}550} \text{ mL}$ of soup leftover.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
George filled $10$ mugs with coffee. He filled each mug with $240\text{ mL}$ of coffee.

**How many *liters* of coffee did George use in total?**  

[[☃ input-number 2]] $\text{L}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
George filled $10$ cups with $240\text{ mL}$ each.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{240}\text{ mL}\times\tealE{10}=~\purpleD{?}$

George used $\purpleD{2{,}400}\text{ mL}$ of coffee.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{240}\text{ mL}\times\tealE{10}=~\purpleD{2{,}400}\text{ mL}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{2{,}400}\text{ mL}$ to liters.

$1 \text{ mL} = \dfrac1{1{,}000}\text{ L}$

So,

$\begin {align}{\purpleD{2{,}400} \text{ mL}}&= {\purpleD{2{,}400}  \text{ 
mL}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ L}}{{1{,}000 \text{ mL}}} 
\\\\
&= {\dfrac{\purpleD{2{,}400}  \cancel{\text{ mL}}}{1}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ L}}{{1{,}000 \cancel{\text{ mL}}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{2{,}400} \text{ L} }{1{,}000}\\\\
&=\purpleD{2{,}400} \text{ L}\div1{,}000\\\\
&=\purpleD{2.4}\text{ L}
\end{align}$
George used $\purpleD{2.4} \text{ L}$ of coffee.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Coach Jill brought $28 \text{ L}$ of sports drink to the soccer game. She divided the sports drink equally between $7$ coolers.  

**How  many *milliliters* of  sports drink did Coach Jill put in each cooler?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{mL}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Coach Jill divided $28 \text{ L}$ into $7$ equal parts.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{28}\text{ L } \div \tealE7 = {}\maroonD{?}\text{ L}$

Each cooler contained $\maroonD4 \text{ L}$ of sports drink.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{28}\text{ L } \div \tealE7 = \maroonD{4}\text{ L}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonD4\text{ L}$ to milliliters.

$1 \text{ L} = 1{,}000\text{ mL}$.

So,

$\begin{align}
\maroonD4\text{ L}&=\maroonD4\text{ L} \times {1{,}000}\text{ mL per L}\\\\
 &= \maroonD{4{,}000} \text{ mL}
\end{align}$
Coach Jill put $\maroonD{4{,}000} \text{ mL}$ of sports drink in each cooler.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Jim has $19{,}700\text { g}$ of sand in his sandbox.  He brings home another $6{,}300\text { g}$ of sand from the beach to add to his sandbox.  
 
**How  many *kilograms* of  sand does Jim have in his sandbox now?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{kg}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Jim had $\blueE{19{,}700}\text { g}$ of sand and added  $\maroonD{6{,}300}\text { g}$ more.  


[[☃ image 1]]



$\blueE{19{,}700}\text { g} + \maroonD{6{,}300}\text { g} = {}\purpleE{?}$

Jim has $\purpleE{26{,}000}\text { g}$ of sand in his sandbox.


[[☃ image 1]]



$\blueE{19{,}700}\text { g} + \maroonD{6{,}300}\text { g} = {}\purpleE{26{,}000}$

We need to convert $\purpleE{26{,}000}\text { g}$ to kilograms.  

$1 \text{ g}=\dfrac1{1{,}000}\text{ kg}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleE{26{,}000} \text{ kg}&=\purpleE{26{,}000}\text{ g}\times\dfrac{1\text{ kg}}{1{,}000\text{ g}}\\\\
&=\purpleE{26{,}000}\cancel{\text{ g}}\times\dfrac{1\text{ kg}}{1{,}000\cancel{\text{ g}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleE{26{,}000}\text{ kg}}{1{,}000}\\\\
&={\purpleE{26{,}000}\text{ kg}}\div{1{,}000}\\\\
&=\purpleE{26}\text{ kg}
\end{align}$
Jim has $\purpleE{26}\text{ kg}$ of sand in his sandbox.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Ally sliced $32 \text{ kg}$ of watermelon for a party.  She divided the watermelon slices equally between $8$ large bowls.
 
**How  many *grams* of  watermelon did Ally put in each bowl?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{g}$


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Ally divided $32 \text{ kg}$ of watermelon into $8$ large bowls.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{32}\text{ kg} \div \tealE8 = {}\maroonD{?}\text{ kg}$

Each bowl had $\maroonD4 \text{ kg}$ of watermelon.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{32}\text{ kg} \div \tealE8 = {}\maroonD{4}\text{ kg}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonD4\text{ kg}$ to grams.

$1 \text{ kg} = 1{,}000\text{ g}$.

So,

$\begin{align}
\maroonD4\text{ kg}&=\maroonD4\text{ kg} \times {1{,}000}\text{ kg per g}\\\\
 &= \maroonD{4{,}000} \text{ kg}
\end{align}$
Ally put $\maroonD{4{,}000} \text{ g}$ of watermelon into each bowl.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
At science camp, the kitchen served half of their blueberries with breakfast.  After dinner, they served the remaining $5{,}270 \text{ g}$ of blueberries with dessert.    

**How many *kilograms* of blueberries did the science camp begin with in total?**   

[[☃ input-number 2]] kilograms


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
The kitchen served half of their blueberries with breakfast and half with dessert. They served $5{,}270\text{ g}$ of blueberries at each meal.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{5{,}270}\text{ g} + \maroonD{5{,}270}\text{ g} = ~\purpleE{?}$

The kitchen served $\purpleE{10{,}540}\text{ g}$ of blueberries.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{5{,}270}\text{ g} + \maroonD{5{,}270}\text{ g} = ~\purpleE{10{,}540}\text{ g}$
The science camp began with $\purpleE{10.54}\text{ kg}$ of blueberries in total.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|

assessment_items.question_content:
It is $3{:}32\text{ p.m.}$ and Emma has gymnastics lessons at $6{:}00\text{ p.m.}$ 

**How many *minutes* are there until Emma's gymnastics lessons?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Let's find out how much time there is between $3{:}32\text{ p.m.}$ and $6{:}00\text{ p.m.}$


[[☃ image 1]]

Let's add minutes until we get to the next hour, $4{:}00\text{ p.m.}$



[[☃ image 1]]



It is $\blueE{28}$ minutes until $4{:}00\text{ p.m.}$

Now, let's find out how many hours are between $4{:}00\text{ p.m.}$ and $6{:}00\text{ p.m.}$

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\maroonD{2}$ hours between $4{:}00\text{ p.m.}$ and $6{:}00\text{ p.m.}$

Now, lets convert $2$ hours and $28$ minutes into only minutes.

$1$ hours = $60$ minutes.

$2\text{ hours}\times60\text{ minutes per hour} = 120 \text{ minutes}$
$120 \text{ minutes} + 28\text{ minutes} = 148\text{ minutes}$
There are $148\text{ minutes}$ until Emma's gymnastics lessons.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Kristen gets home from work at $5{:}34\text{ p.m.}$ Kristen's dinner is ready at $5{:}51\text{ p.m.}$

**How many *seconds* after Kristen gets home is her dinner ready?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] seconds

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Let's find out how many minutes are between $5{:}34\text{ p.m.}$ and $5{:}51\text{ p.m.}$


[[☃ image 1]]



Kristen has $\blueE{17}$ minutes until dinner.
Now, lets convert $17$ minutes into seconds.

$1$ minute = $60$ seconds.

$17\text{ minutes} \times 60\text{ seconds per minute} = 1{,}020 \text{ seconds}$
Kristen's dinner is ready $1{,}020\text{ seconds}$ after she gets home.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
It takes Andrew $720$ seconds to take a shower. He spends an additional $420$ seconds eating breakfast.

**How many minutes does it take Andrew to take a shower and eat breakfast?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] minutes

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Andrew takes $\blueE{720}\text { seconds}$ to take a shower and $\maroonD{420}\text { seconds}$ to eat breakfast.



[[☃ image 1]]



$\blueE{720}\text { seconds} + \maroonD{420}\text { seconds} = {}\purpleE{?}$

It takes Andrew $\purpleE{1{,}140}\text{ seconds}$ to shower and eat breakfast in the morning.



[[☃ image 1]]



$\blueE{720}\text { seconds} + \maroonD{420}\text { seconds} = {}\purpleE{1{,}140}\text{ seconds}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleE{1{,}140}\text{ seconds}$ to minutes.

$1$ second = $\dfrac{1}{60}$ minute.

So,

$\begin{align}
\purpleE{1{,}140}\text{ seconds} &= \purpleE{1{,}140}\text{ seconds}\times\dfrac{1\text{ second}}{60\text{ seconds per minute}}\\\\
&= {\dfrac{\purpleE{1{,}140} \cancel{\text{ seconds}}}{1}}\times \dfrac{1 \text{ minute}}{{60 \cancel{\text{ seconds}}}} \\\\
&= {\dfrac{\purpleE{1{,}140}\text{ minutes}}{60}} \\\\
&=\purpleE{1{,}140}\text{ minutes}\div 60\\\\
&=\purpleE{19}\text{ minutes}
\end{align}$
It takes Andrew $\purpleE{19}\text{ minutes}$ to shower and eat breakfast.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
A butterfly flew $743 \text{ cm}$ and landed on a rose. Then the butterfly flew another $567 \text { cm}$ to go to a daisy.

**How  many *meters* did the butterfly fly in total?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
The butterfly flew $\blueE{743}\text { cm}$ and then flew $\maroonD{567}\text{ cm}$ more.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{743}\text { cm} + \maroonD{567}\text { cm} = {}\purpleE{?}$

The butterfly flew $\purpleE{1{,}310}\text { cm}$ in total.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we need to convert $\purpleE{1{,}310}\text { cm}$ to meters.

$1 \text{ m}=100\text{ cm}$ so, $1\text{ cm} = \dfrac{1\text{ m}}{100\text{ cm}}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleE{1{,}310} \text{ cm}&={\purpleE{1{,}310}}\text{ cm}\times\dfrac{1\text{ m}}{100\text{ cm}}\\\\
&={\purpleE{1{,}310}}\cancel{\text{ cm}}\times\dfrac{1\text{ m}}{100\cancel{\text{ cm}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleE{1{,}310}\text{ m}}{100}\\\\
&={\purpleE{1{,}310}\text{ m}}\div{100}\\\\
&=\purpleE{13.1}\text{ m}
\end{align}$
The butterfly flew $\purpleE{13.1}$ meters in total.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Adam went on a $54 \text { km}$ hike.  He divided the hike evenly over $6$ days.  

**How  many *meters* did Adam hike on each day?**  


[[☃ input-number 3]] meters


[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Adam hiked $54\text{ km}$ over $6$ days.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{54}\text{ km} \div \tealE6 = {}\maroonD{?}\text{ km}$

Adam hiked $\maroonD9 \text{ km}$ each day.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{54}\text{ km} \div \tealE6 = \maroonD{9}\text{ km}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonD9\text{ km}$ to meters.

$1 \text{ km} = 1{,}000\text{ m}$.

So,

$\begin{align}
\maroonD9\text{ km}&=\maroonD9\text{ km} \times {1{,}000}\text{ m per km}\\\\
 &= \maroonD{9{,}000} \text{ m}
\end{align}$
Adam hiked $\maroonD{9{,}000} \text{ m}$ each day.

options.explanation:
Conversions| | | |||
- | | | |
$1$ kilometer $= 1{,}000$ meters| | $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds|| $1$ liter$= 1{,}000$ milliliters|
$1$ meter $= 100$ centimeters| |$1$ hour $= 60$ minutes|| $1$ kilogram $= 1{,}000$ grams|
$1$ meter $= 1{,}000$ millimeters|| ||$1$ gram $= 1{,}000$ milligrams|
$1$ centimeter $= 10$ millimeters|| |||

assessment_items.question_content:
Tessa is training for a marathon. She runs $13\text{ km}$ each day for $3$ days.

**How  many *meters* did Tessa run in total?**  


[[☃ input-number 2]] meters


[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Tessa ran $13\text{ km}$ each day for $3$ days.



[[☃ image 1]]

$ \tealE{3} \times{}\maroonD{13}\text{ km} = ~\purpleD{?}$

Tessa ran a total of $\purpleD{39} \text{ km}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$ \tealE{3} \times{}\maroonD{13}\text{ km} = ~\purpleD{39}\text{ km}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{39}\text{ km}$ to meters.

$1 \text{ km} = 1{,}000\text{ m}$.

So,

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{39}\text{ km}&=\purpleD{39}\text{ km} \times {1{,}000}\text{ m per km}\\\\
 &= \purpleD{39{,}000} \text{ m}
\end{align}$
Tessa ran a total of $\purpleD{39{,}000} \text{ m}$.
